hook: You already know how to solve two equations with two unknowns — but what happens when an engineer needs to balance a system of dozens of variables at once, or a business needs to optimize profit under many constraints? Algebra alone starts to buckle under that weight, and a more powerful language is needed. In this unit, you'll discover how matrices turn complex, multi-layered problems into organized, systematic calculations that computers and mathematicians rely on every day.
concept: The unifying idea is that many real-world problems involve relationships among multiple quantities simultaneously, and matrices give us a compact, structured way to represent, manipulate, and solve those relationships — this is the foundation of *linear algebra*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will sharpen your substitution and elimination skills and extend them to handle more complex two- and three-variable systems with confidence.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Multivariable Linear Systems and Row Reduction
topic_roadmap.description: Master the systematic method of row reduction (Gaussian elimination) to solve linear systems with three or more variables by transforming an augmented matrix into a clean, readable form.
Learn to add, subtract, and scale matrices, and understand the rules that govern when these operations are defined based on matrix dimensions.
Apply the row-by-column multiplication rule to multiply matrices together, and discover why this operation is the engine behind transformations, data processing, and solving systems.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Inverse Matrices and Solving Matrix Equations
topic_roadmap.description: Find the inverse of a square matrix and use it to solve matrix equations of the form $AX = B$, turning a system of equations into a single elegant calculation.
Calculate the determinant of a matrix and use Cramer's Rule to solve linear systems by expressing each variable as a ratio of determinants.
Apply systems of linear inequalities to model real-world optimization problems and find the maximum or minimum value of an objective function within a feasible region.
